name: Pie Charts — Sharing the Whole Circle
whatIsIt: A PIE CHART (circle graph) shows how a whole is split into parts. The full circle is the total (100%, or 360° of turning). Each slice's size shows its share: a bigger slice means a bigger part. To draw a slice we turn its fraction of the total into an angle: angle = (part ÷ total) × 360°.
whyNeeded: Pie charts make shares easy to SEE at a glance — how a monthly budget is spent, how a class voted, or how a day is divided into sleep, school and play. Newspapers, election results and survey reports use them everywhere.
realLifeUses: A family budget split into food, rent, savings and travel
How a class voted for their favourite sport
How your 24-hour day is spent (sleep, school, play, meals)
Market share of phone brands in a survey

vocabulary.meaning: A circle split into slices to show parts of a whole.
One wedge of the circle showing one part's share.
The angle of a slice at the centre; the whole circle is 360°.

story.title: Ravi's Pocket Money
story.text: Ravi gets ₹200 pocket money. He spends ₹100 on books, ₹50 on snacks and saves ₹50. Sir says, 'Draw it as a pie!' Books are half the money, so half the circle — 180°. Snacks and savings are a quarter each, so 90° each. Ravi shades the biggest slice for books and grins: 'One picture shows exactly where my money goes.'
story.extractedProblem: Ravi spends ₹100 of ₹200 on books. What angle should the 'books' slice have in the pie chart?
story.answerInStory: 180° — books are half of ₹200, and half of 360° is 180°.

pies.label: Books = half the circle (2 of 4 quarters)

visual.caption: Ravi's money as a pie: books fill half the circle (180°), with snacks and savings a quarter (90°) each.

teachingGallery.title: Fraction of the circle → angle
teachingGallery.note: Half = 180°, quarter = 90°, and each fraction turns into its own angle.

standardMethod.summary: Turn each part into a fraction of the total, multiply by 360° to get its angle, then draw the slices. To read a chart, do the reverse: angle ÷ 360° × total.
steps: Add all parts to get the total.
For each part, find part ÷ total (its fraction of the whole).
Multiply that fraction by 360° to get the slice's central angle.
Draw each slice with a protractor; check all angles add to 360°.

workedExamples.problem: In a class of 40, 10 like cricket. What angle is the cricket slice?
A slice has an angle of 72° in a survey of 30 people. How many people does it represent?

commonMistakes.mistake: Slice angles that don't add up to 360°.
commonMistakes.fix: All the central angles in a pie chart must total 360° — check your sum before drawing.

commonMistakes.mistake: Multiplying the fraction by 100 instead of 360 to get the angle.
commonMistakes.fix: Percentages use 100, but a pie ANGLE uses 360°. Angle = fraction × 360°.

easy.q: The whole pie chart is how many degrees?
A bigger slice in a pie chart means…

medium.q: Half of the data is one category. Its slice angle is?
8 of 24 students walk to school. Angle of the 'walk' slice?

challenge.q: A slice is 90° in a survey of 20 people. How many people is that?
To find a slice's angle you multiply its fraction by…

questions.q: All slice angles in a pie chart add up to?
A quarter of the data — what slice angle?
12 of 36 like maths. Angle of that slice?
For a pie ANGLE, multiply the fraction by…

teachBackPrompt: With a grown-up, split your day (24 hours) into sleep, school, play and meals. Turn each into a fraction of 24, multiply by 360°, and sketch a pie chart. Explain why all your slice angles must add to 360°.

revisionCard.summary: A pie chart splits a whole circle (360°) into slices. Slice angle = (part ÷ total) × 360°. Bigger slice = bigger share. All angles add to 360°. To read: people = (angle ÷ 360) × total.

meta.curriculum: CBSE Class 8 / ICSE / IGCSE (pie / circle graphs)

rememberIt.hook: Angle = fraction × 360° — the whole pie is 360°
rememberIt.unpack: Percent uses 100; a pie ANGLE uses 360. Slices must sum to 360°.

studentQuestions.q: Pie chart or bar graph — which to use?
studentQuestions.a: Use a pie chart to show PARTS OF A WHOLE (shares). Use a bar graph to COMPARE separate amounts.
A full circle is 360°, so the whole data set fills 360° and each part takes its fair slice of it.

formulas.name: Slice angle
formulas.remember: fraction of the whole, times a full turn

formulas.name: Read a slice
formulas.whenToUse: Finding how much a given slice represents.
